result:
The first time I tasted a persimmon I was twenty-three and living in a city that didn’t love me back.  
The fruit had been left on the windowsill of a sublet in the 19th arrondissement, a four-story walk-up whose elevator had been gutted for copper sometime during the Sarkozy years. I had come to Paris because a woman I barely knew told me my voice would sound better in French, and because I had nothing in particular to lose except the remaining $3,400 of a student loan I’d spent mostly on chemically mellow coffees and second-hand theory books. The lease was for six weeks, the security deposit was half of that money, and the wallpaper exhaled a exhausted yellow, the color of old newsprint someone once thought could cheer a room.

I didn’t know the fruit was a persimmon; I thought it was a tomato that had been insulted by the century. It sat next to a chipped saucer holding two euros in centimes and a condom whose foil wrapper had begun to molt. I ate it like an apple, skin and all, and my mouth folded in on itself, every membrane shrinking from the tannic blast. I stood over the sink, half laughing, half gagging, certain I had just ingested a metaphor. Later, at the marché on Boulevard de la Villette, I learned the word kaki, learned that you wait until the flesh goes almost liquid, that patience is less a virtue than a chemistry experiment. The vendor told me to choose the ones bruised by their own sweetness. I thought: that’s the biography I want.

We think of exile as something that happens across oceans, but there is also the exile of standing in your own kitchen at 3 a.m., unable to name the shape of the hunger that woke you. The small catastrophe of the fluorescent bulb, the refrigerator humming like a distant motorbike. I had come to Paris to write a thesis on Situationist drift—dérive, the deliberate surrender to the city’s currents—but instead I found myself taking the same route every day: up the Buttes-Chaumont, across the suspension bridge engineered by Haussmann’s forgotten hydraulics, down into the Jewish bakery where the owner greeted me with the Hebrew I didn’t know he thought I knew. I bought challah still warm, tore into it while walking, crumbs leaping onto the scarf I would later lose in someone’s studio. The problem with drifting is that the city’s magnetism is not evenly distributed; some pockets pull you into orbit for months, others spit you out before your shadow has time to anchor.

I was, at that point, technically still a Virgin, a fact I carried like an incorrect stamp in a passport. I had read enough Barthes to fear the punctum of my own innocence, and enough Anais Nin to fear the opposite. My inexperience was not theological but statistical: an accumulation of near-misses, aborted messages, evenings that ended in polite coughs. I told myself this was a form of resistance to late-capitalist commodification of intimacy, which is the sort of sentence you can only pronounce aloud in a city where no one asked. But the truth is simpler: I was waiting for permission that would never arrive because I had forgotten to specify whom I’d asked.

Paris gave me corridors instead of answers. I learned which metro doors opened automatically and which required the mercy of strangers. I learned that in August the city belonged to tourists and to非洲兄弟们 who sold warm coke out of backpacks, their French rapid and lilting like a song half-remembered. I learned that if you circled the lake in the Bois de Vincennes slowly enough, retired men would nod you into their chess games, and that the crows there had learned to unzip backpacks in search of sandwiches wrapped in foil. I learned how to pee for free in the Pompidou by walking in like you had a rendezvous on the fifth floor, then taking the escalators all the way up, past the Kandinskys, to the toilets where the attendants no longer checked tickets. Art as alibi, bladder as comrade.

One night in October the woman who’d promised my voice would improve in French appeared at the apartment with a bottle of Armagnac and a cut on her cheek shaped like the Île de la Cité. She wouldn’t explain the blood, only said, “Tonight we’re not translating ourselves.” We drank from floral teacups because I hadn’t bought glasses. She opened my laptop, deleted the first forty pages of my thesis, replaced them with a single line: Le désir n’est pas l’absence du réel, mais le réel en excès. Desire is not the absence of the real, but the real in excess. Then she kissed me against the radiator, and the metal left a ladder of burns on the backs of my thighs. When I yelped, she laughed into my mouth, said pain was just another dialect. We didn’t sleep together; she left at dawn wearing my only belt, claiming she needed to hold something together. I never saw her again, but the next morning the bruises flowered into the exact shade of the persimmons now collapsing in the fruit bowl. I took it as instruction: wait until the bruise sweetens.

I began, then, to collect accidents. A Polish photographer who sprained his ankle on the metro gap and let me carry his tripod all the way to Barbès. A Senegalese student who read my palm in the university cafeteria and refused payment, saying the future should never cost more than the past. A drag queen named Ursula who performed lullabies in the basement of a bar near Château d’Eau, her wig made from the same synthetic fiber as the cheap scarves sold up the street. Each encounter lasted the length of a metro ticket, sometimes two. I wrote their names on the inside of my jacket, a private liner notes of mutual usage. I understood, without being able to articulate, that I was assembling a constellation whose lines would only make sense once I stepped far outside it.

The thesis rewrote itself into something resembling a fragmented guidebook: how to cross a city without arriving, how to preserve the moment before language hardens into meaning. My advisor, a man who wore turtlenecks in July, told me the project had become “promiscuously phenomenological,” which I chose to hear as encouragement. He signed off with a warning: “Don’t forget that even drift maps leave smudges.” I typed his sentence into my notes, then took the 67 bus to the Père-Lachaise, where I left the printed draft on the tomb of an obscure communard whose name I liked the sound of: Eugène Varlin. I imagined him rising at night to read my footnotes, nodding at the passages on surplus value and rim jobs. A kind of after-life peer review.

Winter arrived sideways, a wet cold that crawled inside the fibers of supposedly weatherproof coats. My shoes grew cracks that inhaled water; I learned to stuff them with newspaper, the headlines bleeding into my soles. I spent Christmas Eve volunteering at a church off Ménilmontant—not out of faith, but because they served dinner to people who, like me, had nowhere language felt fluent. The priest was Algerian, his French thick with the sun of Oran. Before the meal he asked us to name one thing we were afraid to lose. Around the table: teeth, visa, mother, passport, room, mind. When my turn came I said “the ability to be surprised.” He translated it into Arabic, wrote it on a slip of paper, tucked it into my palm like a tram ticket. Later, in the small hours, I unfolded it and found he had written something else: the first Arabic verb I ever conjugated: أُحِبُّ / uḥibbu, I love. The paper is still in my wallet, softened by years and the sweat of crossing borders.

By January the city’s gray achieved the consistency of wet cement. I began to fantasize about southern latitudes, oranges, the kind of sun that makes you forgive your own skin. Instead I took a job subletting the room of a Canadian dancer who’d torn her ACL during an audition for the opera. She needed someone to water her plants and forward her mail, payment in the form of rent-free sanctuary. The room was in the Marais, above a falafel stand whose smell rose through the floorboards until my clothes carried the echo of cumin and fried onion. At night I listened to the Orthodox boys sing downstairs, their adolescent voices breaking like waves against the shore of something larger than Saturday. I learned to say Shabbat shalom without feeling like a fraud, learned that the dancer’s succulents required the same negligence I had once mistaken for care in human relationships.

One plant, though, refused the script: a bird-of-paradise that drooped daily toward extinction. I rotated it toward the window, sang to it in the only language whose lullabies I knew by heart: the mangled Hebrew of summer camps I’d never asked to attend. The plant responded by growing a new leaf the shape of a plane ticket. I took it as signal. Packed a small bag, left the rest of my belongings in the dancer’s closet like a chrysalis I half-hoped would finish me in my absence. Took the train south, the slow one that stops at every town whose name contains a silent x. Somewhere after Lyon an elderly man sat across from me, opened a tin of madeleines still warm from his wife’s oven, and offered one without asking where I was from. We ate in silence, the train rocking us like disobedient children. When he stood to exit at Montélimar he pressed the tin into my hands. “Pour la route,” he said. For the road, which is not the same as for the destination.

I got off two stops later at a village whose name I promptly forgot, walked until the pavement surrendered to dirt, then kept walking. The afternoon smelled of thyme and diesel, a combination that felt like forgiveness. I found a grove of olive trees older than the concept of France, their trunks twisted into syllables no longer spoken. Among them a small stone hut, doorless, roof partially caved. I stepped inside, the ground litter with last year’s fruit, purple and shriveled like the knuckles of giants. In the corner: a pile of blankets, a kerosene lamp, a paperback whose cover had been eaten by humidity. I opened it at random: a line about how exile begins the moment you name your longing. I closed the book, lay down, and for the first time in months felt the luxury of not being observed.

I stayed three weeks, maybe four. Time became the shape of tasks: walk to the spring before the sun climbed the hill, boil water for coffee thick as silt, read the warped novel backward to watch the plot unspool into innocence. I spoke aloud to the trees, practicing the future perfect: I will have forgiven, you will have forgotten. At night the cold arrived with a metallic taste, and I wrapped myself in the Canadian dancer’s abandoned shawls, their sequins catching starlight like tiny surveillance devices. I masturbated under the inverted bowl of sky, unsure whether the sound I made was prayer or census. One dawn I woke to find a wild boar staring at me from the doorway, its eyes reflecting my own startled need. We held each other’s gaze long enough for a treaty to be negotiated: I would not covet its olives, it would not covet my dreams. Then it turned, disappeared into the brush with the shrug of someone who has other borders to breach.

When I returned to Paris I found the dancer had come back early, her leg in a purple cast autographed by strangers. She was watering her own plants now, the bird-of-paradise blooming absurdly orange, as if it had spent my absence flirting with the sun. She asked no questions, only handed me a letter that had arrived in my name. Inside: a single persimmon, so ripe it had begun to weep, and a note in handwriting I didn’t recognize: Ton absence a mûri. Your absence has ripened. No return address, no signature. I held the fruit until it bruised the palm of my hand, then ate it over the sink, the flesh collapsing like a small nation surrendering without siege. The sweetness was almost unbearable, a referendum on everything I had not yet dared.

Years later—because that is how we measure time once calendars lose their jurisdiction—I would find myself in a different hemisphere, in a city where the moon appears upside-down. I would be teaching a class on architectural failures, showing students photographs of bridges that collapsed under the weight of their own symbolism. One student, a girl with a tattoo of the Paris metro map across her shoulder blades, asked me if I believed ruins could migrate. I told her about the olive grove, the stone hut, the novel read backward. I told her about the persimmon, how sweetness is just decay that has learned to advertise. After class she handed me a box: inside, wrapped in newspaper, were three fruits hard as promises. “They need time,” she said, “but so do we.” I carried them home on a bus that smelled of eucalyptus and brake fluid, placed them on the windowsill of an apartment that finally contained furniture I had chosen.

I waited. The skin softened, the flesh grew translucent, the tannins relaxed their grip. One evening I cut into the first: the color of sunset spilled across a cutting board scarred by previous decisions. I lifted a piece to my mouth, tasted the entire decade between that initial error and this deliberate correction. Same fruit, different grammar. Outside, the southern sky performed its daily abandonment of light, a slow-motion version of every departure I had ever mistaken for arrival. I thought of the Algerian priest, the dancer’s blooming exile, the boar who taught me the etiquette of trespass. I thought of the woman who deleted my thesis, her mouth tasting of Armagnac and revolution. I thought of all the languages I had borrowed and returned frayed at the edges. And I understood, without grandeur, that we are all just waiting for the bruise to sweeten, for the moment when damage becomes biography, when the mouth that once puckered in refusal learns to shape the syllable of welcome.

The fruit dissolved, leaving only the slick seed in my hand, dark as a metro tunnel. I planted it in a pot on the balcony, knowing it would never grow here—wrong climate, wrong mythology. But I needed the gesture: to bury what had already taught me everything it could. Some maps are drawn in reverse, their accuracy verified only when you trip over the memory years later. I close my eyes and taste the persimmon again, its insistence that sweetness is not the opposite of loss but its apprentice. Outside, the upside-down moon hangs like a careless accent, reminding me that exile ends the moment you pronounce your own name in a dialect that never existed before you spoke it.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
